coredata.dc:title: Development of three-dimensional semi-solid hydrogel matrices for ratiometric fluorescence sensing of Amyloid β peptide and imaging in SH-SY5 cells: Improvement of point of care diagnosis of Alzheimer's disease biomarker
coredata.dc:creator: Hamd-Ghadareh, Somayeh
Salimi, Abdollah
Parsa, Sara
Mowla, Seyed Javad
coredata.dc:description: Alzheimer's is a neurodegenerative disease with high morbidity and mortality in the elderly, so, detection of its biomarker for definite diagnosis of Alzheimer's in the early stage of disease is a challenge. Amyloid beta peptide (Aβ) chosen as an Alzheimer's biomarker. Here, we developed novel, semi-solid, three-dimensional hydrogel matrices for ratiometric fluorescence detection of Aβ. This assay's great performance stems from the employment of a hybrid conjugate composed of Rhodamine B (RB), Carbon dots (CDs), and an Aβ probe entrapped in Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), and then detection of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) that occurs in the presence of AuNP/target-Aβ, as a result of hybridization. The RB-CDs' fluorescence (at 582 nm and 675 nm under 430 nm excitation) is quenched in the presence of AuNPs, while the ratio of fluorescence (I582/I675) is increased by the addition of Aβ target, and shows a linear relationship in the range of 75 pM–250 nM, with a detection limit of 0.5 pM. Furthermore, the assay possesses strong selectivity for Aβ compared to other proteins, and different quantities of a human serum sample successfully analyzed with excellent sensitivity, satisfactory precision, and reliability. Due to distribution of Aβ in SH-SY5 human neuroblastoma cells, extending this UV–Vis–NIR full-range responsive CDs bio-probe to imaging of Aβ in cells. In both fixed and living SH-SY5 cells, the nanoprobe delivers a clear signal to the Aβ target. Because of its high sensitivity, selectivity, biocompatibility and affordability, this nanoprobe is a good option for early Alzheimer's disease diagnosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Alzheimer's
Amyloid beta peptide (Aβ) biomarker
Ratiometric fluorescence assay
Hydrogel film
SH-SY5 human neuroblastoma cells
Multicolor bioimaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The CDs' structure and surface properties
Optical characteristics of CDs solution and solid-state CD's
Aβ detection mechanism, and hydrogel based fluorescent probe fabrication
Optical responses and analytical performance of the bio-probe
Real samples analysis and selectivity study
Cell cytotoxicity and in vivo imaging
In vitro imaging in SH-SY5 cells

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: S.H. Ghadareh acknowledge research office of University of Kurdistan for the Postdoc financial support (Grant Number s-99-11.19304 ). The Iranian Nanotechnology Initiative support is also acknowledged.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The high-resolution TEM images of the (A) CDs and (B) AuNPs (insets are size distribution histogram), (C) XPS scanning spectrum, and (D) Survey scan spectrum of the acquired CDs. (E) The fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of RB and CDs, inset; daylight (left) and UV irradiation (right) photos of green-emitting CDs. (F) Normalized fluorescence and absorbance spectrum for RB, absorbance spectrum for AuNPs and fluorescence spectrum for CDs, showing considerable overlap of RhB emission, CDs emission and AuNP absorbance. The spectral overlap for AuNPs is shown in blue, while that for RB is shown in green. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) The fluorescence spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelengths; (B) 2D excitation-emission counter maps of produced CDs; (C) the fluorescence spectra of RB-CDs hydrogel at different excitation wavelengths. (D) 2D excitation-emission counter maps of the produced RB-CDs hydrogel. (E) Digital photographs of PVA, CDs-PVA and, RB-CDs-PVA film under visible (left side) and UV light (right side). (F) Digital photo illustrating the films' flexibility and transparency.

(A) Scheme for the proposed mechanism of biosensor and its related fluorescence response (B) The linearity of the response curve (intensity ratio of RB-CDs at 582/675 nm versus logarithm of Aβ concentration) and (C) related fluorescence spectra after the addition of different concentrations of Aβ, at an excitation wavelength of 430 nm. (D) Histogram related to the analytical signal of the immunosensor after incubation with various interfering species, including 10 pM of Aβ (a), 0.01 ng mL−1 Bilirubin+ a, 0.01 mg/mL BSA +a, 10 pg mL−1 IgG+ a, 10 fg/mL CA125 + a, 10 fg/mL CA15-3 + a, 10 fg/mL CA19-9 + a. (E) MTT results of SH-SY5 cell viability after coincubation with the RB-CDs (10 μg/mL) at varying times. Blue bars denote the control, and purple bars denote RB-CDs. The data is articulated as mean ± SD from three independent experiments (*p < 0.05). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A), (B) Fluorescence microscopy of live SH-SY5 cells lines pretreated with RB-CD-Aβ probe for 15 min at 37 °C, under the excitation of 488 and 550 nm respectively, the scale bar is 50 μm. (C) The DIC and CLSM images of the SH-SY5 cell lines treated with 10 μg/mL of the RB-CDs for 15 min. The scale bar is 50 μm.
Schematic representation showing (A) the fabrication of CDs, (B) the CDs-based hydrogel sensing platform, and (C) the mechanism of Aβ peptide sensing.
Comparison of the analytical performance of proposed hydrogel based immunosensor with other biosensors for Aβ detection.
Detection of target Aβ in serum samples by the proposed immunosensor.

head.ce:title: Development of three-dimensional semi-solid hydrogel matrices for ratiometric fluorescence sensing of Amyloid β peptide and imaging in SH-SY5 cells: Improvement of point of care diagnosis of Alzheimer's disease biomarker

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Kurdistan, 66177-15175, Sanandaj, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, University of Kurdistan, 66177-15175, Sanandaj, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Nanotechnology, University of Kurdistan, 66177-15175, Sanandaj, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Center for Nanotechnology, University of Kurdistan, 66177-15175, Sanandaj, Iran.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Biological Sciences, Tarbiat Modarres University, P.O. Box: 14115-154, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Biological Sciences, Tarbiat Modarres University, Tehran, Iran.

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Chemistry, University of Kurdistan, 66177-15175, Sanandaj, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Alzheimer's is a neurodegenerative disease with high morbidity and mortality in the elderly, so, detection of its biomarker for definite diagnosis of Alzheimer's in the early stage of disease is a challenge. Amyloid beta peptide (Aβ) chosen as an Alzheimer's biomarker. Here, we developed novel, semi-solid, three-dimensional hydrogel matrices for ratiometric fluorescence detection of Aβ. This assay's great performance stems from the employment of a hybrid conjugate composed of Rhodamine B (RB), Carbon dots (CDs), and an Aβ probe entrapped in Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), and then detection of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) that occurs in the presence of AuNP/target-Aβ, as a result of hybridization. The RB-CDs' fluorescence (at 582 nm and 675 nm under 430 nm excitation) is quenched in the presence of AuNPs, while the ratio of fluorescence (I582/I675) is increased by the addition of Aβ target, and shows a linear relationship in the range of 75 pM–250 nM, with a detection limit of 0.5 pM. Furthermore, the assay possesses strong selectivity for Aβ compared to other proteins, and different quantities of a human serum sample successfully analyzed with excellent sensitivity, satisfactory precision, and reliability. Due to distribution of Aβ in SH-SY5 human neuroblastoma cells, extending this UV–Vis–NIR full-range responsive CDs bio-probe to imaging of Aβ in cells. In both fixed and living SH-SY5 cells, the nanoprobe delivers a clear signal to the Aβ target. Because of its high sensitivity, selectivity, biocompatibility and affordability, this nanoprobe is a good option for early Alzheimer's disease diagnosis.

ce:section.ce:para: Alzheimer's disease appears to be the most prevalent neurodegenerative disorder worldwide, with a huge social cost and no effective treatment to yet (Kawashima and Ihara, 2002; Takahashi et al., 2017), so the development of an effective assay for diagnosis of Alzheimer's in the early stages of the disease is a challenge. Amyloid beta-peptide (Aβ) is a well-known biomarker for Alzheimer's disease (AD) diagnosis (Karki et al., 2021). For quantification of Aβ biomarkers, various analytical methods have been reported, including electrochemical (Qin et al., 2019), enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (Yang et al., 2015), and fluorescence (Zhang et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2018), and so on. Fluorescence analysis is a safe and effective analytical technique that may be utilized in a range of biological and biomedical applications, such as biosensing, cellular and in vivo imaging, medication, and therapies (Li et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2019; Zhou et al., 2020; Kobayashi et al., 2010). Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) action would improve sensor sensitivity by increasing the effectiveness of emission contrast by environmental cues owing to the emission energy transferred from donor to acceptor in bi fluorophores. As a result, the FRET feature is widely used in sensor applications such as medical diagnostics, biological analysis, and optical imaging (Gao et al., 2021).

Conventional fluorescence-based sensors typically perform their assigned duties by counting on concentration-dependent fluorescence maximum peak amplification or quenching, which is frequently restricted by other substances and uncontrolled variables like instrument voltage and concentration variation (Li et al., 2019). Ratiometric fluorescence sensors, on the other hand, feature two emission peaks that may be cross-referenced to provide a self-calibration system that eliminates external effects while increasing sensitivity to target analytes (Liu et al., 2020). The fluorescent nanoprobe based on “FRET” and “ratiometric” mechanisms was considered the most appealing analytical instrument for disease diagnosis using different fluorescence probes due to its high selectivity (Bunet and Wouters., 2017). Traditional organic fluorescence probes have a high fluorescence quantum yield and strong light stability, but they also have significant drawbacks (Wan et al., 2020).

Carbon dots (CDs) are being investigated as a potential alternative to traditional fluorescent semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), which have a number of drawbacks, including heavy metal toxicity, probable health and environmental hazards, high price, and biocompatibility (Li and Li, 2020). Other related features of CDs are simplicity of surface functionalization, large-scale production, and photobleaching protection (Wang et al., 2021). CDs, unlike QDs, are uncomplicated to cross-link and have excellent adjustable fluorescence characteristics, making them ideal for a variety of applications, especially as fluorescent probes for metal ion detection, pH, temperature, and bio-molecular sensing (Qaddare and Salimi, 2017; Hamd-Ghadareh et al., 2017; Mohammadi et al., 2018). In accordance with such properties, this unique type of fluorescent nanomaterial surpasses quantum dots (high toxicity issue) (Ruzycka-Ayoush et al., 2021) and traditional dyes (photobleaching) (Yang et al., 2018).

These CD-based nanoprobes are commonly dispersed and stored in a liquid media, making long-term storage and transfer problematic. Because of energy transmission between neighboring CDs particles, these nanoprobes experience aggregation-caused quenching (Li et al., 2019). Despite the fact that most CD-based fluorescence sensing recognizes analytes in aqueous solution, many research groups recently come a long way in developing gel-based or solid-state sensor systems (Heo et al., 2011) and CDs can be immobilized within hydrogel matrices for sensing applications (Zhu et al., 2016; Mohammadi et al., 2021).

Hydrogels or polymer films with porous 3-dimensional matrices have been applied for medical drug delivery, food additives, biomaterials, diagnostics, and biomolecule separation, and their potency for environmental sensing has only recently been studied (Ji et al., 2019). When fluorescent nanoparticles are functionalized and incorporated into a hydrogel matrix, a three-dimensional matrix is created with the ability to interact with water-soluble analytes, and the produced gel makes quantitative sensing easier (Date et al., 2020). The use of such engineered hydrogels as active sensor networks is a novel direction being investigated for sensing because they have the potential to improve either solid or liquid substrates or combine the advantages of both (Petryayeva et al., 2013).

Additional advantage of utilizing hydrogels as a biosensing support matrix is the existence of pores, which not only improve effective surface area but also promote diffusion of the analyte from the outward solution to the inside sites, providing optimal and quick access to the analyte. Localization of the analyte is enabled by well-designed hydrogels with pore sizes that match the target size, resulting in a much-improved detection procedure. The utilization of CDs dispersed in a solid matrix to make a composite material also seems to be a good potential way to address the aforementioned issues (Nayak et al., 2020). The use of hydrogels combined with conjugated fluorescent CDs as sensor networks might alleviate particle agglomeration, inconsistent analyte adsorption, and the possibility of liquid frameworks having a reduced shelf life (Wu et al., 2018). Furthermore, the use of CDs to incorporate fluorescent dyes is a straightforward method of improving fluorescence sensors' efficiency (Liu et al., 2020; Diac et al., 2015). The conjugation of dye molecules with CDs, in particular, would not only prevent aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching of dye molecules, but also maintain their stability and chemical activity, resulting in a useful platform for practical applications (Wan et al., 2020).

In this study, we developed a solid ratiometric FRET-based smart hydrogel probe for detecting and quantifying Aβ peptides and in vitro/vivo imaging of SH-SY5 neuroblastoma cells. To set up an immunocomplex test targeting Aβ, we used fluorescent RB-conjugated CDs in a PVA-stabilized hydrogel matrix and AuNPs as acceptors in the FRET process. The ability of these fluorescent hydrogel composite materials modified with Aβ-antibody to selectively interact with Aβ is described. At 430 nm excitation, the RB-CDs nanocomposites emitted 582 nm and 675 nm intensities. Due to the tight coordination between RB-CDs and Aβ antibody, the red emission decreased after the addition of Aβ peptide, while the green emission increased and the ratio of I582/I675 altered with changing the Aβ concentration. Interestingly, the proposed probe exhibits selectivity towards Aβ peptide compared to other abnormal protein aggregates, and the proposed assay was used to detect Aβ peptide in human serum with satisfactory results.
The applied chemicals, various instruments which have been used for characterization and measuring, and also the synthesis procedure of hydrogel, CDs, and AuNPs are reported in the supporting file.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The CDs' structure and surface properties
ce:section.ce:para: TEM images were used to examine the shape and morphology of the CDs. As seen in Fig. 1 A, the CDs are distributed evenly. According to the TEM image, the prepared CDs have a uniform size range of 3–7 nm, with an average particle size of 3.8 nm (the size distribution histogram inserted). Also, as illustrated in Fig. 1B, the TEM image of AuNPs indicates a uniform size range of 5–20 nm (the size distribution histogram inserted). The chemical structure and surface functional groups of CDs, were investigated using FTIR (Fig. S1A). The prepared CDs include a variety of absorption bands, comprising O–H and N–H stretching vibrations with a broad peak at 3418 cm−1 and 3382 cm−1 while the C–H stretching vibration has a narrow peak at 2976 cm−1. The FTIR data show that certain functional groups, such as –COOH, –OH, and –NH2, are present on the surface of the CDs. The XPS spectrum of CDs for composition analysis showed in Fig. 1C and D. The relative atomic percentages of C, N, and O in the produced CDs product were reported in Table S1, according to the findings. In the high-resolution C1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 1D, Fig. S1B), the peaks at 284.8 eV, 285.5 eV, 286.2 eV, and 287.0 eV correspond to C–C/CC, C–N, C–O, and CO, accordingly, that is comparable with the FTIR results. As shown in Fig. 1E, there was a large proportion of overlap between the emission spectrum of RB and the excitation spectrum of CDs, which was beneficial to the energy transfer between RB and CDs. Fig. 1E inset displaying photos of the CDs solution under sunlight (left) and under 365 nm UV irradiation (right) which shows the photos of green-emitting for CDs. In an ideal situation, FRET happens between a donor and an acceptor with a significant spectral overlap (the donor's emission spectrum and the acceptor's absorption spectrum). The PL spectrum of RB-CDs (black line in Fig. 1F), which comprised emissions at 487 nm, 582 nm, and 675 nm, exhibited good spectral overlap with the UV–Vis absorption of AuNPs, with a sharp peak centered at around 520 nm (blue line in Fig. 1F) and RB with sharp absorption maximum at 560 nm (green line in Fig. 1F). This data implies substantial FRET emerges between fluorescence donor RB-CDs and the acceptor AuNPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical characteristics of CDs solution and solid-state CD's
ce:section.ce:para: Extreme accumulation of CDs in a film or solid-state powder decreased the fluorescence efficiency which limited their advancement in solid-state applications such as latent fingerprints and photoelectric devices (Zhang et al., 2019; Y. Wang et al., 2020). To begin with, CD solid state emission is primarily in the blue green area, with little long-wavelength emission (Zhao and Tan., 2021). Second, solid-state fluorescence CDs with long-wavelength emission usually have a low QY, typically just under 25% (Xu et al., 2020). Third, the vast majority of solid-state CDs emit a unique maximum. As a result, to prepare long-wavelength-emissive CDs featuring high-efficiency solid-state fluorescence and dual emission, a simple synthesis platform is required. For extremely emissive solid-state CDs and to develop a FRET assay for Aβ peptide detection, here CDs with dual-emission and high-throughput solid-state fluorescence were combined with a PVA hydrogel.

The fluorescence spectra of CDs illustrated in Fig. 2 A. By increasing excitation wavelength from 310 to 500 nm, the first emission, red-shifts from 470 to 512 nm and under visible irradiation going to disappearing, whereas the second emission at near infrared region is nearly constant (Fig. 2A). The excitation-dependent behavior of CDs originates from intrinsic structure, intricate surface states and dissimilar particle sizes (Alamet et al., 2015; Amao and Komori., 2004). The emission at near-infrared region originates from the porphyrin derivatives exist in CDs (Zheng et al., 2008). The 2D excitation-emission counter maps of produced CDs illustrated in Fig. 2B, as its obvious there are two distinct photoluminescence centers at 478 (accounting for a minor fraction of the total) and 675 nm (the major emission peak) under a wide range of excitation wavelengths, respectively.

The PL spectra of RB-CDs-hydrogel were examined at various excitation wavelengths to demonstrate the connection between the emissions (Fig. 2C). The RB-CDs fluorescence spectra comprise three distinct emissions maxima which the emission of CDs at 478 nm moves to a longer wavelength when the excitation wavelength increased. CDs can emit fluorescence in the range from UV to near-infrared (NIR) and dual emission wavelengths by varying the excitation wavelength from 320 to 450 nm was observed. The visible excitation wavelength is of particular interest in fluorescence biosensing due to the low biodegradation effects compared to UV irradiation. Hence, the excitation wavelength of 430 nm was used as the optimal excitation wavelength (Fig. 2C).

Under the identical slit circumstances, at the excitation of 430 nm, the emission at 478 nm for CDs nearly fades and the greatest emission was found for RB-CDs (emissions at 582 nm and 675 nm, Fig. 2A and C). Fig. 2D shows the profile of PL spectra for RB-CDs-hydrogel by varying excitation wavelength from 280 to 460 nm. As can be seen in the contour map of RB-CDs in Fig. 2D, triple emission with distinctive features comparable to RB-CDs suspension, is observed. For characterization of the produce hydrogel film the photograph of PVA, CDs-PVA and, RB-CDs-PVA film under visible and UV light irradiation is shown (Fig. 2 E). As illustrated under visible light the color of the hydrogel film changed from gray to yellow and brown when hydrogel is modified with Cds and CDs-RB, while under UV illumination the observed color for CDs-PVA and RB-CDs-PVA hydrogel were green and red. Furthermore, the observed digital photo for RB-CDs-PVA hydrogel (Fig. 2F) illustrating the films' flexibility and transparency. Even after 2 months of storage in typical laboratory conditions, the films keep their flexibility.

The interaction between CDs and RB in the hybrid is a key factor that influences the efficiency of the response of ratiometric fluorescence sensors; hence, UV–Vis absorption spectra are used to evaluate the connection between CDs and RB. Fig. S2A shows the absorption spectra for RB-CDs-Aβ antibody solution and RB-CDs-Aβ antibody hydrogel. The prepared CDs have strong UV–Vis absorption at approximately 403 nm and 673 nm (Fig. S2A), and there is an absorption band of RB from 325 nm to 375 nm, as shown in Fig. S2A. Because of the absorption bands overlap, the dual emissive ratiometric fluorescent sensor may be made using just one excitation wavelength. The absorption peak at 403 nm is come from the n−π* electronic transition of CO groups (Ding et al., 2020). By the way, the additional absorption peak at 673 nm originated from porphyrin derivatives (Long et al., 2020). The comparison of UV–Vis spectra of solution and hydrogel state CDs nanohybrids implies that solid state hybrids have prominent comparable absorption bands. Following integrating with PVA, however, the intrinsic barrier of the strong interactions of π-stacking in the conjugated system produces an insignificant reduction in the absorbance bands. CDs have an absorption peak at 403 nm and 673 nm, whereas RB has absorption peaks at 350 and 550 nm, respectively, in the mixed solution. There are no new peaks, implying that CDs and RB are still connected as they were. Fig. S2B shows the absorption spectrum of AuNPs and AuNPs-Aβ antibody, a noticeable shift in the intensity and maxima of absorption was seen with the immobilization of antibody on AuNPs (Zhang et al., 2012), suggesting the effective stabilization of antibody on AuNPs (Fig. S2B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Aβ detection mechanism, and hydrogel based fluorescent probe fabrication
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed fluorescent probe is composed of inter-reactive fluorescence arrays. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , the FRET responding strategy was used to design an innovative ratiometric hydrogel-based platform, which uses a hybrid structured PVA entrapped fluorophore (RB-CDs) as a donor and AuNPs as an acceptor. For the aforementioned purposes, RB was utilized as the reference unit, performing as a built-in calibration to remove background interferences and environmental factors in order to produce a dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence-detecting platform. Fig. 3 A depicts the suggested ratiometric energy transfer mechanism for the fluorescent probe. The FRET pathway was altered once RB was included in this nanoprobe, making energy transfer easier. Second, the CDs' and RB's fluorescence can both be excited by the same excitation wavelength. However, due to its chemical inertness, the fluorescence emission wavelength of RB can stay unchanged even in the presence of Aβ (Hu et al., 2021).

The absorption and emission of RBs converged significantly with the emission and absorption of CDs, implying that RB molecules, acting as mitigating factors, can alter emission trends. Intramolecular FRET is induced by RB molecules and the FRET performance improved by increasing CD navigation in a fixable configuration through a hydrogel network and also energy transfer in a sequential manner.

On the other hand, there was a large proportion of overlap between the emission spectrum of RB, the absorption spectrum of AuNPs and the excitation spectrum of CDs, which was beneficial to the FRET between AuNPs and CDs, so, the fluorescence of CDs is more strongly reduced in the presence of AuNPs due to enhanced spectral overlap. Along with the electrostatic coupling between CDs (positively charged) and AuNPs (negatively charged), the antibody modified-CDs assemble with AuNPs biospecifically. The bio-specific localization of AuNPs around CDs leads to fluorescence quenching at 675 nm and an increase in the rigidity of the nanostructure. Afterwards, as a result of this phenomenon, RB molecule fluorescence at 582 nm recovered steadily due to the increase in the rigidity of the nanostructure and the formation of the immunocomplex between antibody modified-CDs, Aβ, and antibody modified-AuNPs (CDs-AB/Aβ/AB-AuNPs).

The proposed FRET nanoprobe acts ratiometrically (I582/I675), meaning that with an increase in Aβ concentration, the probe emission at 582 nm increased steadily and the second emission at 675 nm decreased significantly due to strong energy transfer through the FRET phenomenon in the presence of Aβ and antibody modified AuNPs (Scheme 1).

To begin, an RB-CDs-Aβ antibody was produced and integrated with PVA (Scheme. 1A, B). To design and assemble bio-probe RB (0.15 ng mL−1) and CDs (50 μg mL−1) at proper ratios were covalently conjugated. Then, Aβ antibody (1 μM) was bio-conjugated to the carboxyl group of CDs via EDC/NHS chemistry. Then, the fluorescent probes were encompassed in a PVA framework by H-bonding interactions, electrostatic interactions and also physical entanglement and Aβ antibody-conjugated CDs were distributed in the hydrogel. In the next step, a prepared solution of the Aβ peptide (target) at different doses in a 2:1 (v/v) solution of DMSO/water and at 37 °C, was incubated for 1 h. By the introduction of AuNPs the FRET of the RB-CDs-AuNPs arrays will enhanced (Scheme. 1C) and some sandwich-assemblies formed. As a result, the collected ratiometric fluorescence signal is proportional to the target concentration, and this approach may be used to detect of Aβ.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical responses and analytical performance of the bio-probe
ce:section.ce:para: Solid state films of fluorescent hybrid housed at PVA was kept at 4 °C until needed. The experiments were carried out on solid phase film made from a hydrogel solution comprising 10 mL of RB-CDs-Aβ antibody reaction solution, 50 μL AuNPs, and various concentrations of target Aβ (5 μL). The effect of diffusion time of the analyte to the hydrogel was investigated by recording of PL after addition of the analyte to the hydrogel. As illustrated in Fig. 3S the optimum time for analyte diffusion is about 60 min (Fig. S3), so, the ratiometric fluorescence intensity (I582/I675) was recorded after 60 min. Following introducing various amounts of Aβ solution, using 430 nm to excite and emission spectra have been obtained as shown in Fig. 3C. The green emission of fluorescent probe at 582 nm increased steadily with the introduction of Aβ from 75.0 pM to 250.0 nM, while the red emission intensity at 675 nm decreases dramatically, providing a tool for performing a ratiometric (I582/I675) analysis of Aβ concentration variation. The results of the assay revealed that the ratiometric fluorescent probe correlates very well with changes in Aβ concentration at the proposed concentration range (Fig. 3B), with a great linear relationship (y = 2.3508 x + 23.747, R2 = 0.9905) and a LOD of 0.5 pM. These results showed that the designed probe might be a potential sensor for detecting Aβ. Furthermore, the approach used in this study was identical to other previously published methods for identifying Aβ, which are summarized in Table 1 A. Compared to earlier published approaches, the developed system in this study features a LOD that is equivalent to or better than previous methods, a great linear range, and a relatively fast reaction time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real samples analysis and selectivity study
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility of the bio-probe for measurement of Aβ in real samples was assessed through spike of human blank serum with different concentrations of targeted Aβ. As can be seen in Table 1 B, the detection performance was excellent. As demonstrated in Table 1B, sample recoveries ranged between 101% and 103%, illustrating the test's reliability and accuracy and dependability in real samples.

Inspired by the above experimental results, such as high sensitivity and good LOD of the fabricated bio-probe, we attempted to examine the possibility of detecting the Aβ content in the presence of potential interference. The biosensor's selectivity was assessed by measuring the intensity ratio of the immunoassay signals for recognizing various amounts of bilirubin, BSA, IgG, CA125, CA15-3 and CA19-9 (Fig. 3D). Furthermore, the selectivity of the nanoprobes without hydrogel matrix was also studied. As illustrated in the Fig. S4 the interfering effect of the proposed interferences is negligible. The impact of the test species content of these interfering agents on the probe is negligible. These test results were fairly inspiring, showing quite good potential for application of the constructed FRET ratiometric fluorescent probe for detecting Aβ in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell cytotoxicity and in vivo imaging
ce:section.ce:para: Cell viability of SH-SY5 cells was evaluated in the presence of 10 μg/mL of RB-CDs which incubated with all of the cells. As illustrated in Fig. 3E, the blue bars represent the control, whereas the green bars represent 10 μg/mL of RB-CDs at various time periods (6, 12, 24 and 48 h) respectively. The viability of SH-SY5 cells is only reduced by roughly 6% when RB-CDs are added, as shown in Fig. 3E. As a result, the fluorescent probes developed have low cytotoxicity and high biocompatibility, making them suitable for bioimaging of living cells or other therapeutic applications.

CDs exhibit high biocompatibility, which makes them more suitable than other staining agents such as organic dyes, fluorescent proteins, and (semiconductive) metal-based quantum dots for biolabeling applications. Hydrophilicity of CDs has been reported to be a vital factor for the labeling of organelles. In addition, their excellent photostability allows long-term monitoring of dynamic cellular processes. Excitation wavelength-dependent emission properties of fluorescent CDs provide advantages of multicolor imaging of cells or organelles. Fig. 4 A, and B shows the inverted fluorescence microscopy images of live SH-SY5 cell lines pretreated with RB-CD-Aβ probe for 15 min at 37 °C, under the excitation of 488 and 550 nm respectively, the scale bar is 50 μm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro imaging in SH-SY5 cells
ce:section.ce:para: Encouraged by the excellent results of the in vivo cell imaging, we further investigated the in vitro detection in fixed SH-SY5 cells. The model cells were incubated with RB-CDs probe (10 μg/mL) for 15 min. Then, the treated SH-SY5 cells were imaged under different windows. The dynamic properties of cellular membranes have a strong effect on the interaction and endocytosis of the CDs. RB-modified Aβ antibody functionalized CDs, have been shown to penetrate into SH-SY5 cells through the endocytosis mechanism, with features of targeting of cytoplasm as well as possessing a promising fluorescence. As shown in Fig. 4C, differential interference contrast (DIC) and confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) images of treated SH-SY5 cells incubated with a fluorescent probe presented bright green, yellow, and red fluorescence, which suggested that the RB-CDs probe had penetrated into the cells and accumulated in the cytoplasm. The overlay image showed a bright yellow view, suggesting the excellent ability of the designed probe for monitoring the distribution of Aβ peptide in SH-SY5 cells. The RB-CDs probe acts as an efficient carrier that greatly targets the uptake efficacy of the SH-SY5 cells.

Blue fluorescence may be found on the majority of the CDs. Using blue-emitting CDs was not an option since cells and bio-tissues also produce blue fluorescence, which creates spectrum interference. As a result, the problem may be solved by using long wavelength emission. For the development of a FRET-based hydrogel structure, we employed a straightforward fluorescent technique by using a long-wavelength emissive CD. Under the excitation of 430 nm, the binary system of RB-CDs housed at PVA revealed dual-emission of 582 nm and 675 nm. A ratiometric fluorescent signal was elicited when Aβ was conjugated with the fabricated bio-probe. The immunoassay demonstrated excellent sensitivity regarding Aβ with a wide linear range from 75.0 pM to 250.0 nM and a limit of detection of 0.5 pM. The high sensitivity might be attributed to the immobilization of an Aβ specific antibody in the microporous nanostructure and counter peptide's configuration, the peptide's ability to redirect for effective binding, greater antibody surface concentration, and/or the marker's improved diffusion regime. In addition, in both fixed and living neuroblastoma SH-SY5 cells, the nanoprobe delivers a clear signal to the Aβ target. The biosensor's additional benefits included its simplicity, low cost, and absence of interference, which might be used to diagnose Aβ biomarker at the point of care.

Somayeh Hamd-Ghadareh: Doing the experiment and, Writing – original draft. Abdollah Salimi: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Sara Parsa: Cell cytotoxicity experiments and data analysis. Seyed Javad Mowla: Taking the Cell bioimaging.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: S.H. Ghadareh acknowledge research office of University of Kurdistan for the Postdoc financial support (Grant Number s-99-11.19304). The Iranian Nanotechnology Initiative support is also acknowledged.
